text: We are just looking at the bladder neck size. Is it okay or you would like to reconstruct that or what is your take on this?
I think we will need to reconstruct it but I think we will need to reconstruct it because we had too many stitches posteriorly and too close to each other.
Had we placed the stitches a little bit further, we might not need to reconstruct it. We will see.
So sometimes we use a six and a nine o'clock position, six inch and a nine inch.
We didn't use that because I thought the bladder neck would be smaller, and it probably would have been, but we came too close in the posterior side.
So, always the concern about uretic orifice, really do you make any attempt to look at it or you just say, well, take it as... So, the concern is more concerned than a real issue.
The surgeon inspects the ureteric orifices while making an incision at the bladder neck.
The surgeon places an RB-1 suture in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a small stitch to perform the anastomosis, ensuring that the stitch does not come close to the ureteric orifices (UOs) after cutting the bladder neck.

The surgeon cuts the bladder neck close to the ureteric orifices in a patient with lateral lobes, then reconstructs the bladder neck in a fish-mouth shape to ensure the ureteric orifices are recessed and free from the anastomosis.
Other people, Dr. Bhandari, and I'd ask Ethan what he would do, They would not. They would reconstruct it, not reconstruct it laterally, just do the anastomosis the way it is.
The surgeon places an additional stitch at the bladder neck, visible as the needle passes through the tissue, securing the area around the ureteric orifices.
Yeah, I mean it looks like you'll have to do an anterior tennis racket.

I'll tell you that I've changed my ways a little bit in that I almost always reconstruct and I make a really very small bladder neck, probably twenty-eight, thirty French opening at greatest dimension. Has not shown to make any functional outcome difference.
But yeah, I almost always reconstruct even small bladder necks like this one and make it really very small.

I asked would you to do the anastomosis because the way we run it is we run two rooms. By this time, I'm starting the next room. And so I am a little rusty about the anastomosis. I need to do about five or ten to get up to speed.

So I probably would have placed the stitches further in the posterior aspect and I would not have reconstructed this but this is the way Vuju does it and you know there's no difference in outcomes as Ketan mentioned.
The surgeon uses a fenestrated grasper, visible on the opposite side, to manipulate tissue during the prostatectomy procedure.

Now, at one point of time, one of the mentors told us that we should use two needle holders. What is your take on that, really? It's better to use two needle holders. It's more natural to use two needle holders.
I thought we would have to use them because the pelvis is narrow. But this is cost-saving. I mean, we've used this grasper for the dissection.
And so, you know, that's two hundred bucks less or ten thousand rupees less. Twenty thousand rupees less. So certainly a cost-cutting measure, I guess, on table.
But it is tougher because the feel is different. You use more force holding the needle holder.
You have to use less force for the grasper. With the needle holder, you can sew with both hands. With the grasper, you can sew with both hands, but you've got to be very precise in how you hold it.

So, Vuj is doing a fantastic job. I don't think that he needed the second needle holder and he just saved the hospital and the patient twenty thousand rupees. Yes, of course. Thank you very much for that really.

The one point which I wanted to ask you was, One of the mentors did show us while you are coming into the bladder neck it is possible that at the posterior or towards the capsule of the bladder you enter and at mucosa you enter at a different level because of the curve. Would that happen sometime?
In fact, quite the contrary, I've made an attempt to go sharply through so that you have the full thickness of the bladder. If you bevel it, your stitches might not hold. So a point that

that I try to make, and I learned this from Guino and Valencia, is look at how thick the anterior bladder wall is. Incise the anterior wall sharply so you know how thick it is, and then incise the posterior bladder wall to that degree. Don't angle it. So I would normally tell people when they use the hook that instead of using the hook straight, wrist it so that it is right angled to the posterior dorsal wall.
